text: The surgeon observes a grossly distended gallbladder with a green appearance, indicating gangrenous changes. The gallbladder is decompressed for easier handling, followed by elevation and initiation of blunt dissection.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator as a retractor to manage bleeding caused by dense inflammatory reactions during dissection. The dissection occurs high on the gallbladder wall, with lateral manipulation to retract the gallbladder medially and laterally, aiding in achieving the critical view.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum along the undersurface of the suspected cystic duct within the hepatocystic triangle, performing dissection up the lateral right side of the gallbladder. The gallbladder appears edematous and friable, with visible gangrenous changes to its wall.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to assist in elevating the gallbladder and opens the peritoneum to expose the junction of the cystic duct and the hepatocystic triangle. The bottom of the gallbladder is gradually revealed as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon identifies the high initial peritoneal incision and joins it, using the suction irrigator to maintain the proper plane. The hepatocystic triangle is being cleared, revealing the cystic artery and the junction of the cystic duct with the gallbladder. The surgeon elevates the bottom of the gallbladder to achieve the critical view, with the posterior view of the cystic duct visible.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to develop a window around the cystic duct, clearing lymphovascular tissue to improve retraction. The backside of the cystic duct is cleaned, and a window is created in the hepatocystic triangle. The surgeon addresses bleeding from the artery caused by the maneuver, ensuring not to take the cystic duct until achieving a clearer critical view. Blunt dissection is primarily used to separate tissues.

The surgeon carefully thins out lymphovascular tissue holding the gallbladder to ensure no ductal or vessel structures are present. After achieving a complete critical view, the cystic duct is clipped and divided, allowing for the removal of the gallbladder from the liver bed.

The surgeon carefully separates posterior vessels from the gallbladder to avoid bleeding, while detaching the gallbladder from the liver edge. The gallbladder is used as a retractor before being completely separated from the liver. Hemostasis is achieved in the liver bed, followed by irrigation, before amputating and extracting the gallbladder.
Patient did well, was discharged the same day. I hope you enjoyed.
